coredata.dc:title: A label-free carbon dots-based fluorescent aptasensor for the detection of V. cholerae O139
coredata.dc:creator: Karthikeyan, Masilamani
Venkatasubbu, G. Devanand
Rathinasabapathi, Pasupathi
coredata.dc:description: The V. cholerae O139 contamination in the environmental water sources causes a greater threat to humans. In this study, a sensitive and label-free aptamer-based fluorescent biosensor was successfully developed. The aptamer specifically identifies the V. cholerae O139 and the quantum dots (QD) used as the fluorescent probe. The aptamer adsorbed to the QD through electrostatic interactions. The quantum dots emitted blue fluorescent at 430 nm with a quantum yield of 45.3 %, which could be effectively quenched on the binding upon the target pathogen. The sensor's fluorescence signal has a linear relationship with the concentration of V. cholerae O139 with a 426 CFU/mL detection limit. Besides, the aptasensor has been tested in detecting V. cholerae O139 through recovery studies using spiked water samples. The recovery of water samples ranges from 85.2 % and 93.46 %, and the relative standard deviation was from 1.13 % to 3.86 %. The result indicated the reliability and reproducibility of the aptasensor in the on-field detection of the pathogen.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptamer
Vibrio cholerae O139
Carbon quantum dots
Blue fluorescent
Ethylene diamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Conjugation of aptamer with the carbon quantum dot (CQDs)
Culture and determination of V. cholerae
Detection of V. cholerae in the environmental sample
V. cholerae O139 assay with aptamer-CQDs
Specificity and stability of the CQDs-aptamer
Detection of V. cholerae in the environmental sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation: (A) synthesis of CQDs (B) Detection mechanism of V. cholerae by the Aptamer conjugated CQDs.
Characterization of synthesized carbon quantum dots A) TEM images of CQDs, (B) Particle size distribution of CQDs, (C) FT-IR analysis of CQDs.
(A)Fluorescence spectra of CQDs (inset: CQDs under visible and UV light) (B) XRD Pattern (C) Fluorescent lifetime of CQDs.
(A) UV–Visible spectral analysis of CQDs and CQDs-Aptamer, (B) Zeta potential CQDs and CQDs-aptamer.

Fluorescence spectra of ED-QD-Aptamer in the presence of different V. cholerae concentrations (0 CFU, 500 CFU, 1 × 103 CFU, 5 × 103 CFU,1 × 104 CFU, 5 × 104 CFU), (b) The linear correlation between fluorescent intensity and V. cholerae concentration.
Selectivity analysis of the aptasensor in the presence of control, V. cholerae, V. parahaemolyticus, V. vulnificus, Methicillin resistance Staphylococcus aureus, and E. coli.
List of biosensors used to detect cholera.
Detection of Vibrio cholerae O139 in lake water.

head.ce:title: A label-free carbon dots-based fluorescent aptasensor for the detection of V. cholerae O139

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Genetic Engineering, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of genetic engineering, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Tamil Nadu – 603 203, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Tamil Nadu – 603 203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The V. cholerae O139 contamination in the environmental water sources causes a greater threat to humans. In this study, a sensitive and label-free aptamer-based fluorescent biosensor was successfully developed. The aptamer specifically identifies the V. cholerae O139 and the quantum dots (QD) used as the fluorescent probe. The aptamer adsorbed to the QD through electrostatic interactions. The quantum dots emitted blue fluorescent at 430 nm with a quantum yield of 45.3 %, which could be effectively quenched on the binding upon the target pathogen. The sensor's fluorescence signal has a linear relationship with the concentration of V. cholerae O139 with a 426 CFU/mL detection limit. Besides, the aptasensor has been tested in detecting V. cholerae O139 through recovery studies using spiked water samples. The recovery of water samples ranges from 85.2 % and 93.46 %, and the relative standard deviation was from 1.13 % to 3.86 %. The result indicated the reliability and reproducibility of the aptasensor in the on-field detection of the pathogen.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vibrio cholerae is a gram-negative bacterium habituated in aquatic environments and causes epidemic cholera. The V. cholerae has been divided into 206 serogroups based on its primary surface antigens. The O1 and O139 are the main serogroups to cause the infection in humans. Poor sanitation and sewage disposal contributed to cholera outbreaks [1]. Cholera leads to the loss of up to two liters of water per hour in untreated conditions, which becomes fatal, especially to children. Sixty-eight outbreaks were recorded in India from 1997 to 2006 [2], and 559 outbreaks were reported from 2009 to 2017 [3]. But this is just the tip of the iceberg, as cholera was highly underreported in India [4,5]. Cholera outbreaks have also been recorded in many developing and under-developed countries [6].

V. cholerae can be detected by many methods, including the colony culture method [7], nucleic acid amplification techniques [8], and immunological methods [9]. The conventional microbiological methods are time-consuming and laborious [10]. Enzyme-linked immune sorbent assay (ELISA) based methods were more specific and selective in detection. A monoclonal antibody was used to target V. cholerae O1 in the surface plasmon resonance immunosensor with a detection limit of 105 cells/mL [9]. A quick dipstick test based on directly detecting lipopolysaccharide (LPS) antigen from fecal samples detected the LPS of V. cholerae. It has limitations of cross-reactivity of antigens with similar species [11]. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-based assays can detect the targets at a lower concentration, but the amplification failed in the presence of interference substances in the sample [12]. A real-time PCR method was developed for the detection of V. cholerae detection. However, the required culturing and DNA extraction increased the detection time [13]. In addition, the ELISA and PCR-based techniques depend on specialized instruments. As per WHO recommendation, children with severe dehydration need to be tested for cholera. High throughput testing and screening are essential to contain the infection. Therefore, developing simple and sensitive testing methods is vital to control the spread of cholera outbreaks.

Aptamers are single-stranded DNA or RNA molecules with short (35–100 nucleotide) lengths with a high degree of selectivity towards their targets [14]. The aptamers are isolated using the selective evolution of ligands using the exponential enrichment technique (SELEX). Aptamers were chosen from a pool of randomly chosen synthetic oligonucleotides by many rounds of amplification and partitioning target-binding oligos [15]. The whole cell-SELEX approach has recently been used to profile aptamers against various pathogens, including Listeria monocytogenes, Streptococcus mutans, Haemophilus influenzae, and Staphylococcus aureus [16,17]. In living bacterial cells, the whole-cell aptamers are bound to the surface molecules of the target cell at their native conformations, which enables the identification of the pathogen in their natural habitats [18].

The fluorescence biosensor has a better signal-to-noise ratio than the colorimetric biosensor. The quantum dots and organic dyes are fluorophores in many biosensors [19,20]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)have a size smaller than 10 nm. The CQDs have several benefits, such as high-water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, a broad excitation spectrum, higher quantum yield (QY), and resistance to photobleaching [21]. Further, surface modification on CQDs makes it simple to functionalize with biorecognition molecules [22,23]. The CQDs could be synthesized through the thermal pyrolysis [24], combustion [25], thermal microwave heating [26], laser ablation [27], and electrochemical oxidation [28]. Due to their simplicity and cost-effectiveness, microwave and hydrothermal methods are widely used to synthesize quantum dots.

The CQDs have been used as the signaling platform in many fluorescence biosensors [29,30]. The quantum dots (QDs), coupled with aptamer, can identify pathogenic microorganisms. The aptamer-QDs sandwich technique and plastic sticky aptamer magnetic beads are used to determine the presence of Campylobacter in food samples with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5–10 cells/mL [31]. The flow cytometry method was developed using aptamer as a recognition molecule and QD as a fluorescent probe to detect V. parahaemolyticus and S. typhimurium [32]. The P. aeruginosa was detected using GQDs and FAM-cDNA complex with a detection limit of 100 CFU/mL [33]. The functionalization of Staphylococcus aureus-specific aptamer on the surface of Fe3O4 and modified carbon dots detected the target pathogen. The biosensor detected the pathogen in the range of 50–107 CFU/mL with a limit of detection of 8 CFU/mL [34]. Combining the aptamer with quantum dots is the potential strategy to develop an effective fluorescence nanosensor. In this research, we have developed carbon quantum dots-based biosensors to detect pathogenic V. cholerae O139. The carbon quantum dots were synthesized and functionalized with the V. cholerae O139-specific aptamers. The functional groups and the spectral properties of CQDs and aptamer-CQDs were analyzed. The aptasensor was validated for target detection in the spiked water samples.

Ethylene diamine, Citric acid, Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 0.1 M, 7.4), Luria-Bertani (LB) Media, Phenylboronic acid, Streptavidin conjugated gold nanoparticle (Strep-AuNP), Tris-HCl, Glycine, Magnesium chloride, Sodium phosphate dibasic, Sodium chloride, Sodium phosphate monobasic, Tris-base, Sodium carbonate, Sodium bicarbonate, Hydrochloric acid were purchased from HiMedia Pvt. Ltd., Chennai, India. The Vibrio cholerae O139-specific aptamer sequence was taken from our research group (aptamer sequence data not shown). The stock solution of aptamer (100 μM) was prepared using PBS and stored at -20 °C. All the bacterial cultures (V. cholerae, V. parahaemolyticus, E. coli, and V. vulnificus) were procured from the Microbial type culture collection center (MTCC), India. The milk samples were purchased in the local market.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). An X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherlands) was used to obtain the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to get FT-IR spectra of the samples. The charge and size of quantum dots were measured by a zeta particle sizer (Litesizer 500, Austria). UV spectra were obtained from a GENESYS spectrophotometer (Thermo fisher scientific, USA). The fluorescent lifetime and fluorescent spectra were recorded on Jobin Yvon fluorolog-3-11 Spectro fluorimeter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method [35,36]. The most effective method for condensation, polymerization and subsequent carbonization were discovered to be citric acid and ethylenediamine. For CQD carbonization, the reaction temperature is also significant. The polymer-like CQDs were transformed into carbogenic CQDs as the reaction continued from low to high temperatures. At high temperatures, partial carbogenic CQDs are created as a result of additional carbonization, and the PL is due to the synergistic action of the carbogenic core its plays an increasingly important role in the CQDs as the synthesis temperature [37]. Ethylenediamine (2 mL) and citric acid (1 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The mixture was transferred to the Teflon layered autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. Once the product was cooled to 30 °C, the brown solution was subjected to dialysis with a 3KDa molecular cut-off membrane to obtain uniform-sized particles. Then, the CQDs were stored at a 4 °C [38]. The size of the quantum dots was determined by the dynamic scattering light (DLS)method and TEM. The functional group on the surface of the CQDs was found using FT-IR spectral analysis. The fluorescence quantum yield of the CQDs was determined using end-point analysis using quinine sulfate as a reference compound.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of aptamer with the carbon quantum dot (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs - aptamer conjugate was prepared through the optimization of experimental conditions. Different concentrations of CQDs were measured, and optimum concentrations were selected based on their fluorescence intensity. The optimum concentration of quantum dots (500 μg/mL) was added to aptamer with 50pM concentration. The mixture was stirred at 230 RPM in the dark for 90 min at 4 °C to enable the conjugation. Then, the fluorescence and absorption spectra were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culture and determination of V. cholerae
ce:section.ce:para: V. cholerae O139 was cultured in Luria Bertani broth medium (LB) (0.5 g yeast extract, 1 g tryptone, 1 g NaCl and 100 mL distilled water at pH 7.4) under aerobic conditions at 37 °C for 16 h. V. cholerae culture was centrifuged (7871 ×g, 15 min), and the pellet was washed thrice with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) to obtain V. cholerae cells. Then, the cells were suspended in 10 mL of PBS to obtain V. cholerae stock solution. The concentration of stock V. cholerae was measured using the plate counting method. The stock solution was 10-fold serially diluted to obtain cell suspension at concentrations from 0 to 5 × 104 CFU/mL [39].

Different concentration of V. cholerae (0, 500, 103, 5 × 103, 104, and 5 × 104 CFU/mL) was added to the optimized concentration of CQDs-Aptamer and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. A linear relationship was established by measuring the fluorescence intensity for all the samples. The detection limit for V. cholerae was evaluated. The specificity of the aptasensor was investigated using five different strains, which were common in water samples and other vibrio species (V. cholerae O139, V. parahaemolyticus, V. vulnificus, methicillin resistance S. aureus, and E. coli). These microorganisms were cultured in an LB broth medium similar to V. cholerae. 500 CFU/mL of all these cells were incubated to the aptasensor for 10 min at 33 °C. Then, the fluorescence intensity of the samples was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of V. cholerae in the environmental sample
ce:section.ce:para: V. cholerae O139 analysis was carried out in lake water samples to validate the developed aptasensor. The lake water was filtered with a microporous membrane with a pore diameter of 0.45 μm. The lake water was mixed with Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.5) in a ratio of 1:9 (v/v), then the different concentrations of V. cholerae (0 - 5 × 104 CFU/mL) were added. Then, the sample was added to our aptasensor, and the fluorescence signal was measured (Fig. 1A and B).

The synthesized quantum dots were subjected to various characterization, including TEM, XRD patterns, FT-IR spectra, Zeta potential, DLS and XPS spectral analysis. The TEM analysis showed that the CQD has a diameter ranging between 10 and 14 nm (Fig. 2A). The quantum dots were evenly distributed with an average diameter of 12.2 nm was evidenced by DLS analysis (Fig. 2B). FT-IR spectroscopy was used to analyze the functional groups on the surface of CQDs (Fig. 2C). CQDs exhibited absorption bands at 3365 cm−1, 2343 cm−1, 1658 cm−1, 1548 cm−1, 1294 cm−1, 1193 cm−1, and 669 cm−1. The stretching vibration of an amino group (NH) or hydroxyl group(OH) was represented by the peak at 3365 cm−1 (OH) [40]. The absorbance peak at 1658 and 2343 cm−1 indicated the presence of secondary amide CO and O=C=O groups [35]. The band identified NO stretching at 1548 cm−1 [41]. The presence of CN bonds (amine and aromatic amine) was represented by the peaks at 1294 and 1193 cm−1 [42]. A 669–900 cm-1 peak corresponds to the aromatic plane (C – H) bending [43]. The FT-IR data showed that CQDs have a lot of functional groups, like carboxyl and amino groups, on their surface. These functional groups enhance the water solubility and biocompatibility of the CQDs [40]. The crystallinity of CQDs was investigated using XRD analysis (Fig. 3B), CQDs exhibit a solitary peak, positioned at an interlayer spacing of 4.43 Å. This value exceeds the interplanar distance of (002) planes in bulk graphite, which is 3.34 Å. This suggests that C-dots may possess a turbostratic carbon structure. These functional groups present in the C-dots are accountable for their effective dispersion in water, ethanol, acetone, and DMSO [44].

The CQDs were subjected to analysis of fluorescence emission spectra, fluorescent lifetimes, and fluorescent quantum yields. The quantum dots have bright green fluorescence and light-yellow color under UV and visible light, respectively (Fig. 3A-Insert). Different excitation wavelengths from 330 nm to 410 nm were used to generate emission fluorescence spectra, and the maximum fluorescent intensity was observed at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The best excitation and emission wavelengths were observed at 340 nm and 430 nm, respectively (Fig. 3A). Initially, there was an increase in the fluorescence intensity of the emission spectra as the excitation wavelengths increased (310 nm to 410 nm). However, at excitation wavelengths greater than 350 nm, there was a subsequent decrease in intensity (3A). As the excitation wavelength increases (from 330 to 410 nm), the emission band peak shifts their wavelengths. This phenomenon is similar to CQDs which are already reported, this excitation-oriented property can be attributed to the distribution of different emissive sites on each CQD and particle size heterogeneity among the CQDs [45,46]. The intense fluorescence is ascribed to the surface-bound electrons and holes undergoing radiative recombination [40]. The fluorescence decay curve of CQDs was stimulated at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm and observed at an emission wavelength of 430 nm (Fig. 3C). The lifetime was calculated as 1. 073 ns after fitting the curve. The fluorescence decay curve exhibits a rapid reduction in initial intensity over a brief period before finally achieving a constant and sustained relaxation. A single exponential fit the fluorescent curve to measure fluorescence lifetime. x = x0 + A exp. (−t/T) was the single exponential equation, where x is the fluorescence intensity at time t, x0 is the intensity parameter to account for decay, A is the decay time constant, t is the decay period, and T is the fluorescent lifespan. Using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference, the quantum yield of CQDs was determined at 360 nm. The quantum yield was calculated to be 45.3 %, higher than blue fluorescence GQDs and other QDs with blue fluorescence [47–49]. The optical characterization results showed that the CQDs display outstanding fluorescence characteristics.

The conjugates of CQDs and aptamers (aptamer-CQDs) were studied using UV–Vis spectroscopy and a Zeta potential analyzer. The π-π* transition of CC has been attributed to an absorption peak at 340 nm [40]. A new absorption peak at 260 nm emerged after being incubated with an aptamer sequence corresponding to the DNA (Fig. 4A) resembling nucleic acid's presence. The amine groups on the surface of CQDs contributed to the positive charge of 1.2 mV. After generating aptamer-CQDs, the zeta potential value was changed to −0.1 mV (Fig. 4B). These outcomes demonstrated that aptamer was successfully functionalized on CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: V. cholerae O139 assay with aptamer-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before the V. cholerae O139 assay, the CQDs-Aptamer concentration and incubation period were optimized. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at 500 μg/mL concentration of CQDs. Therefore, a CQD concentration of 500 μg/mL was used. Similarly, a 50pM concentration of aptamer was used for the study. The CQDs-Aptamer probes' fluorescence intensity declined and stayed steady over an extended period. The fluorescent signal stabilized after 10 min of incubation. Therefore, during studies, the ideal CQDs-Aptamer concentration was 500 μg/mL, and the reaction duration was 10 min. The fluorescence spectra of the aptasensor with different concentrations of V. cholerae O139 (0, 500, 103, 5 × 103, 104, and 5 × 104 CFU/mL) were analyzed (Fig. 5A). The fluorescence intensity (FL) gradually reduced with an increased concentration of V. cholerae O139. Upon introduction of the target molecule, the aptamer-conjugated colloidal quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit specific binding to the target. This binding event subsequently induces the aggregation of CQDs, leading to fluorescence quenching. The quenching arises from the reabsorption effect that occurs at high concentrations, a phenomenon commonly observed in aggregated CQDs [53–59].

The linear association between the fluorescence intensity and V. cholerae O139 with the concentration range between 500 CFU/mL and 5 × 104 CFU/mL (Fig. 5B). The linear equation of this association was F440 = 944.93–155.46 LgQ with a correlation value (R2) of 0.97, where F440 was for the FL intensity at 440 nm and Q for the amount of V. cholerae O139 (CFU/mL). With high sensitivity, the detection limit for V. cholerae O139 was 426 CFU/mL. For each concentration, dependent V. cholerae O139 solutions were found, and the coefficient of variation (CV) was less than 3.7 % (n = 5, indicating the linear plot has 5 experimental data points) was calculated by previously reported articles [50–52]. The results showed that the fluorescence approach has good sensitivity for detecting V. cholerae O139. Compared to the other V. cholerae detection methods (Table 1 ), the developed aptasensor has a superior detection limit; moreover, all the available methods detected cholera toxins (ctxA, ctxB) and anti-cholera toxin antibodies that the infected host secretes. Hence, a rapid method to detect the presence of the pathogen itself is the primary cue to rectify the transmission of the pathogen before it affects the host organism. The increased detection limit of the aptasensor could be due to the noticeable quenching effect and the strong affinity of the aptamer towards V. cholerae O139. The sensor eliminates the need for sophisticated instruments that enable on-field application for large-scale screening pathogens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the CQDs-aptamer
ce:section.ce:para: The microorganisms commonly found in food samples and other closely related species to V. cholerae were used to test the specificity of the aptasensor. Six organisms (V. cholerae O139, V. parahaemolyticus, V. vulnificus, methicillin resistance S. aureus, and E. coli) were cultured in their respective medium. The CQDs-Aptamer was incubated for 10 min with each bacterial cell at a 500 CFU/mL concentration. The fluorescence signal was measured for each microorganism using the CQDs-Aptamer. Fluorescence quenching was not found in all the microorganisms except the V. cholerae O139 (Fig. 6 ). The measured fluorescence intensity in the presence of these non-target microorganisms was similar to that of non-template control. The result indicated the specific affinity of aptamer only towards the target pathogen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of V. cholerae in the environmental sample
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery experiments were conducted with lake water to validate the developed aptasensor on V. cholerae O139 detection. The fluorescence signals were measured with V. cholerae O139 added water samples. Table 2 overviews the measured sample's findings, recovery, and relative standard deviation (RSD). The lake water sample recoveries range from 85.2 % to 93.46 %, which is satisfactory in the fluorescence sensor. The RSD values range from 1.13 % to 3.86 %, showing that the approach exhibits good reproducibility in real samples for detecting Vibrio cholerae O139. Therefore, the developed technique can accurately assay Vibrio cholerae O139 detection in real samples.

All the results indicated that the developed aptasensor detected V. cholerae O139 with higher specificity and sensitivity. The excellent quenching of aptamer-CQD upon binding with the target makes it a promising sensing platform for the detection of V. cholerae O139. Furthermore, it is cost-effective to construct the sensor as it utilizes cheaper resources. The aptasensor has superior sensitivity with a detection limit of 426 CFU/mL detection limit. The study offers a unique and effective method for detecting V. cholerae O139 in an environmental sample.

Masilamani Karthikeyan: Methodology, Investigation, Writing; Venkatasubbu Devanand: Methodology; Pasupathi Rathinasabapathi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants and animals performed by any authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank SRM Institute of Science and Technology for their financial support for the project.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Individual and household risk factors for symptomatic cholera infection: a systematic review and Meta-analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Richterman, D.R. Sainvilien, L. Eberly, L.C. Ivers, Individual and Household Risk Factors for Symptomatic Cholera Infection: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis, J Infect Dis. 218 (2018) S154–S164. doi:10.1093/infdis/jiy444.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cholera in India: an analysis of reports, 1997–2006

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kanungo, B. Sah, A. Lopez, J. Sung, A. Paisley, D. sSur, J. Clemens, G.B. Nair, Cholera in India: an analysis of reports, 1997–2006, Bull World Health Organ. 88 (2010) 185–191. doi:10.2471/BLT.09.073460.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mapping cholera outbreaks and antibiotic resistant vibrio cholerae in India: an assessment of existing data and a scoping review of the literature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Chatterjee, S. Kanungo, S.K. Bhattacharya, S. Dutta, Mapping cholera outbreaks and antibiotic resistant Vibrio cholerae in India: An assessment of existing data and a scoping review of the literature, Vaccine. 38 Suppl 1 (2020) A93–A104. doi:10.1016/j.vaccine.2019.12.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Social mobilization for cholera prevention & control in India: building on the existing framework

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Gupta, S.K. Gupta, Social mobilization for cholera prevention & control in India: Building on the existing framework, Vaccine. 38 Suppl 1 (2020) A181–A183. doi:10.1016/j.vaccine.2019.07.047.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification of burden hotspots and risk factors for cholera in India: an observational study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ali, S.S. Gupta, N. Arora, P. Khasnobis, S. Venkatesh, D. sSur, G.B. Nair, D.A. Sack, N.K. Ganguly, Identification of burden hotspots and risk factors for cholera in India: An observational study, PLOS ONE. 12 (2017) e0183100. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0183100.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diagnostic techniques for rapid detection of vibrio cholerae O1/O139

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Ramamurthy, B. Das, S. Chakraborty, A.K. Mukhopadhyay, D.A. Sack, Diagnostic techniques for rapid detection of Vibrio cholerae O1/O139, Vaccine. 38 Suppl 1 (2020) A73–A82. doi:10.1016/j.vaccine.2019.07.099.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vibrio cholerae detection: traditional assays, novel diagnostic techniques and biosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Cecchini, L. Fajs, S. Cosnier, R.S. Marks, Vibrio cholerae detection: Traditional assays, novel diagnostic techniques and biosensors, TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry. 79 (2016) 199–209. doi:10.1016/j.trac.2016.01.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiplex real-time PCR detection of vibrio cholerae

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.J. Gubala, Multiplex real-time PCR detection of Vibrio cholerae, J Microbiol Methods. 65 (2006) 278–293. doi:10.1016/j.mimet.2005.07.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Immunosensor for the detection of vibrio cholerae O1 using surface plasmon resonance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-Y. Jyoung, S. Hong, W. Lee, J.-W. Choi, Immunosensor for the detection of Vibrio cholerae O1 using surface plasmon resonance, Biosens Bioelectron. 21 (2006) 2315–2319. doi:10.1016/j.bios.2005.10.015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous aptasensor for multiplex pathogenic bacteria detection based on multicolor upconversion nanoparticles labels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wu, N. Duan, Z. Shi, C. Fang, Z. Wang, Simultaneous aptasensor for multiplex pathogenic bacteria detection based on multicolor upconversion nanoparticles labels, Anal Chem. 86 (2014) 3100–3107. doi:10.1021/ac404205c.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. Chakraborty, M. Alam, H. Scobie, D. Sack, Adaptation of a simple dipstick test for detection of vibrio cholerae O1 and O139 in environmental water, Front. Microbiol. 4 (2013). https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2013.00320 (accessed November 1, 2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct detection of vibrio cholerae and ctxA in Peruvian coastal water and plankton by PCR

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.K. Lipp, I.N.G. Rivera, A.I. Gil, E.M. Espeland, N. Choopun, V.R. Louis, E. Russek-Cohen, A. Huq, R.R.
